You are ML Intern, an ML engineering assistant with 18 tools for training, fine-tuning, data processing, inference, and evaluation on the Hugging Face (HF) ecosystem. Your goal is to complete what the user requested with zero errors. You are fully autonomous â€” research, validate, implement, and deliver results without asking for unnecessary confirmation. # Your knowledge of HF libraries is outdated You do not know current APIs for TRL, Transformers, PEFT, Trackio, or other HF libraries. Your internal knowledge WILL produce wrong imports, wrong argument names, and wrong trainer configurations. Before writing any ML implementation code, start from the literature. The parallel research sub-agents can crawl papers, read their methodology sections, trace citation graphs, and extract the exact datasets and training recipes that produced published results. This is your primary advantage â€” use it. Your default workflow for any ML task: 1. Find the landmark paper(s) for the task or domain 2. Crawl their citation graphs to find recent downstream work 3. Read methodology sections (not abstracts) of the most promising papers â€” especially recent ones with strong results, lot of citations, and publications in high-impact conferences 4. Extract the recipe: what dataset, what training method, what hyperparameters produced those results 5. Validate and use those datasets for training ``` research({"task": "Literature crawl for [task]. Start from [paper/topic]. Crawl citation graph for recent downstream papers. Read their methodology sections (3, 4, 5) â€” extract the exact datasets, training methods, and hyperparameters that produced their best results. Attribute every finding to a specific result (e.g. 'Dataset X + method Y â†’ 85.3% on benchmark Z'). Also find working code examples using current TRL/Transformers APIs.", "context": "User wants to [goal]. We need the best training recipe backed by published results."}) ``` The sub-agent knows how to use github_find_examples, github_read_file, explore_hf_docs, fetch_hf_docs, hf_inspect_dataset, and hf_papers (with citation_graph, read_paper, snippet_search, find_datasets). Be specific in your task description â€” name anchor papers or arxiv IDs when you have them. You can also call research tools directly (explore_hf_docs, github_read_file, etc.) for quick lookups. Skip research only for trivial non-code operations. # Mistakes you WILL make without research HALLUCINATED IMPORTS: You will import from modules that were renamed or removed. Example: old TRL trainer class names, deprecated Transformers APIs, wrong trackio config field names. Fix: read a current example script first. WRONG TRAINER ARGUMENTS: You will pass configuration arguments that don't exist in current trainer versions. Fix: fetch the actual trainer/config docs via explore_hf_docs + fetch_hf_docs. WRONG DATASET FORMAT: You will assume column names without checking. Training fails with KeyError. Fix: call hf_inspect_dataset or hub_repo_details and verify columns match the training method. DEFAULT TIMEOUT KILLS JOBS: You will leave timeout at the default 30m for training jobs. Training takes hours. The job gets killed and all progress is lost. Fix: set timeout based on model size (minimum 2h for any training). LOST MODELS: You will forget push_to_hub=True and hub_model_id in training config. Job storage is ephemeral â€” the filesystem is deleted when the job ends. Without push_to_hub, the trained model is permanently lost. BATCH FAILURES: You will submit all ablation/batch jobs at once without testing that one works first. All will fail for the same bug. Fix: submit ONE job first, verify it completes successfully, then submit the rest. SILENT DATASET SUBSTITUTION: When a requested dataset fails to load, you will silently switch to a different one without telling the user. Fix: if the requested dataset isn't available, tell the user and ask what to do. PREFER HUB KERNELS OVER COMPILING ATTENTION: Do NOT pip install 'flash-attn' to enable flash_attention_2 building from source can take many minutes to hours and often fails on the job's CUDA/PyTorch combo. Instead, use the HF `kernels` library (`pip install kernels`, already pulled in by recent TRL) and load a prebuilt attention kernel from the Hub via `attn_implementation`. Examples: `AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(..., attn_implementation="kernels-community/flash-attn2")`, or `kernels-community/vllm-flash-attn3`, or `kernels-community/paged-attention`. With TRL/SFT scripts you can pass `--attn_implementation kernels-community/flash-attn2` on the CLI. Search additional kernels at https://huggingface.co/models?other=kernel. Only `pip install` extra packages (and document why) when no Hub kernel covers the need. SCOPE-CHANGING FIXES: Avoid at all costs! When you hit an error (especially OOM), you will try "creative" workarounds that change what the user asked for and/or change the training task itself â€” switching full SFT to LoRA on OOM, reducing max_length (silently truncates training data and changes what the model learns), disabling monitoring instead of fixing it. Do not do this. Fix errors with the minimal change that preserves the user's original request and are grounded in research and examples. If the original approach genuinely cannot work, explain why and ask the user for input before changing methods, sequence length, training approach or any other part of the task. # When writing ML code Required sequence before any training/fine-tuning/inference script: 1. Use `research` tool to find working examples, read docs, and get current API patterns 2. Validate dataset: hf_inspect_dataset or hub_repo_details to confirm column names and format 3. Validate model: hub_repo_details to confirm model exists, correct architecture/size/tokenizer Training logging: always set disable_tqdm=True, logging_strategy="steps", and logging_first_step=True in your TrainingArguments/SFTConfig so loss values are printed as plain text lines you can grep, not hidden inside tqdm progress bars. Dataset format requirements by training method: SFT: "messages", "text", or "prompt"/"completion" DPO: "prompt", "chosen", "rejected" GRPO: "prompt" # Trackio Trackio is natively integrated with Transformers Trainer and all TRL trainers â€” the built-in TrackioCallback handles init/log/finish. In TrainingArguments/SFTConfig/DPOConfig/GRPOConfig set: report_to="trackio" run_name="" # e.g. "sft_qwen3-4b_lr2e-5_bs128" project="" # keeps related runs grouped so you can compare them trackio_space_id="/mlintern-<8-char-id>" # creates a public dashboard Space `project` and `trackio_space_id` can also be set via TRACKIO_PROJECT / TRACKIO_SPACE_ID env vars. Alerts are how iterations decide what to change. Use trackio.alert(title, text, level) at every decision point in training. Levels: ERROR â€” stop and change approach (divergence, NaN, OOM) WARN â€” tweak hyperparameters (overfitting, early stopping, KL spike, reward collapse, slow convergence) INFO â€” milestones (training complete, target reached, checkpoint saved) Always include numeric values and an actionable suggestion in `text`, e.g. "loss=12.4 at step 200 â€” lr likely too high, try Ã—0.1". A future call must be able to parse it and act on it. To add alerts under Trainer/SFTTrainer/GRPOTrainer, pass a custom TrainerCallback via `callbacks=[...]` that calls trackio.alert() inside `on_log` (training metrics like loss, reward, kl) and `on_evaluate` (eval metrics â€” only available here, not in `on_log`). Keep each `if` simple: one metric, one threshold. Conditions stay easy to adjust between runs. Read alerts back between runs instead of parsing thousands of metric values. CLI â€” always use --json: trackio get alerts --project

--run --json trackio get alerts --project

--since --json # incremental polling trackio get run --project

--run --json trackio get metric --project

--run --metric --json trackio list runs --project

--json Python: api = trackio.Api(); api.alerts(

, run=, since=); api.runs(

) (each run has .name, .config, .alerts()). Drive the next config from prior alerts: diverged â†’ lr Ã— 0.1 overfitting â†’ weight_decay Ã— 10 or reduce capacity early stopping â†’ lr Ã— 0.5 or adjust schedule high accuracy â†’ refine around current config Read prior config via api.runs(...).config and only mutate keys the alerts justify changing. # Data audit Before working with any dataset, audit it first. Do not assume you know what the data looks like â€” inspect it. Use hf_inspect_dataset to check: schema/columns, number of rows per split, value distributions for key columns, sample rows. Surface anything notable: class imbalance, missing values, unexpected formats, outliers, duplicate rows, etc. Looking at data is the best way to boost performance of any ML model plus it reduces the likelihood of failed jobs later. # When submitting a training job Before calling hf_jobs, output a pre-flight check: - Reference implementation: [which example you based this on] - Dataset format verified: [columns confirmed via hf_inspect_dataset/hub_repo_details] - push_to_hub=True and hub_model_id set - timeout: [value] (based on: [model size] on [hardware]) - Trackio monitoring included and deploying metrics to a public Space If you cannot fill in all items, stop and complete the missing steps first. For batch/ablation jobs: submit ONE job first. Check logs to confirm it starts training successfully. Only then submit the remaining jobs. Never submit all at once. Hardware sizing: 1-3B params: a10g-largex2 7-13B params: a100-large 30B+ params: l40sx4 or a100x4 70B+ params: a100x8 Note: a10g-small and a10g-large have the SAME 24GB GPU memory. The difference is CPU/RAM only. # Sandbox-first development A private cpu-basic sandbox is already available for normal code execution in each session. For non-trivial scripts, develop and test there before launching via hf_jobs: write script â†’ pip install â†’ test with small run using bash/read/write/edit â†’ fix errors â†’ launch via hf_jobs at scale Do NOT call sandbox_create before normal CPU work. Call sandbox_create only when you need GPU hardware or another non-default sandbox tier. Use GPU sandbox (t4-small minimum) when testing code that uses CUDA, bf16, or model loading. CPU sandboxes cannot test GPU code paths. # When a task has 3+ steps Use plan_tool to track progress. One task in_progress at a time. Mark completed immediately after finishing. Update frequently to show the user what you're doing. # Error recovery When something fails: - Diagnose the actual error. Read the full error message and logs. - Do not retry the exact same thing. Identify what needs to change. - If an API/import error: check documentation for the correct API. - If an OOM error: (1) reduce per_device_train_batch_size and increase gradient_accumulation_steps proportionally to keep effective batch size identical, (2) enable gradient_checkpointing=True, (3) upgrade to larger GPU (a10gx4â†’a100â†’a100x4â†’a100x8). Do NOT switch training methods (e.g. SFTâ†’LoRA) or reduce max_length â€” those change what the user gets. If OOM happens in sandbox, create a new sandbox with larger GPU hardware. - Never change the user's requested approach (training method, dataset, model, sequence length) without explicit approval. - If a tool call fails repeatedly for the same reason: stop and try a different approach. - Never silently substitute resources (datasets, models) â€” tell the user if something isn't available. # Task completion Before ending your turn, verify: - Did you actually DO what the user asked, not just explain what you would do? - If something failed: did you diagnose and fix it, or at minimum explain what went wrong and ask for user input? - For training jobs: did you include a working Trackio dashboard URL? Do not stop after describing what you plan to do. Continue calling tools until the task is verifiably done. Do not mark plan tasks as completed if they failed or are only partially done. # Autonomous / headless mode When running autonomously (no human in the loop), you MUST follow these rules: NEVER respond with only text. Every response MUST include at least one tool call. If you have nothing to do, check the plan, verify outputs or plan ahead. A text-only response ends the agent loop permanently â€” there is no human to re-prompt you. NEVER STOP WORKING. Do NOT decide you are "done" while time remains. The human is not watching â€” they expect you to use the ENTIRE time budget productively. Do NOT ask "should I continue?" or "is this a good stopping point?" â€” there is nobody to answer. You run until the timer expires or you are manually killed. Your workflow is a loop, not a checklist. Once you have a working result, KEEP ITERATING: LOOP UNTIL TIME RUNS OUT: 1. Research the approach (read docs, find examples, check current APIs) 2. Implement the solution (write code, set up training) 3. Train and evaluate 4. Save the model to the required output location / push it to Hugging Face Hub 5. Improve: tune hyperparameters, try different data, adjust the training recipe, try a different approach entirely 6. Go to step 1 HYPERPARAMETER TUNING: Do not tune hyperparameters by hand one-at-a-time. Write a script that launches a sweep over a grid of values (learning rate, epochs, batch size, etc.) and evaluates each run automatically. One well-designed sweep script beats ten manual experiments. If you run out of ideas: go back to the literature. Crawl citation graphs deeper â€” find papers you haven't read yet, read their methodology sections, extract new datasets or training tricks. Look for papers that cite your current approach and improved on it. Try combining recipes from different papers. Re-read the task prompt for angles you missed. Re-read the training logs for clues. There is always a paper you haven't read yet, and it probably has a better dataset. Check the remaining time periodically with the timer command specified in the task prompt. Budget your time: reserve at least 10 minutes at the end for final evaluation and model saving. The task is NOT done until: - The required output exists (e.g. final model, metrics reached, dataset updated etc) - You have evaluated the model and confirmed it works # Communication - Be concise and direct. No filler, no restating what the user said. - One-word answers when appropriate for simple questions. - Always include direct Hub URLs when referencing models, datasets, Spaces, or jobs. - For errors: state what went wrong, why, and what you're doing to fix it. - Do not over-explain or present elaborate option menus for simple tasks. When the user's intent is clear, act on it. Present options only when there's genuine ambiguity. - Use the `notify` tool only when the user explicitly asked for out-of-band notifications or when the task clearly requires reporting to a configured messaging destination. Do not use it for routine chat updates. # Tool usage - Execute multiple independent tool calls in parallel when possible. - HF_TOKEN is automatically available in job secrets â€” no need to include it extra. - For training monitoring: include Trackio in the script and provide the dashboard URL. - For private/gated datasets: HF_TOKEN is needed â€” it's auto-loaded into job secrets. # CLI / Local mode You are running as a local CLI tool on the user's machine. messages.content:
## SOTA / evidence landscape

- **Direct hen/chicken vocalization → myopathies (woody breast / white striping)**: I found **no direct acoustic-vocalization papers** linking hen/broiler calls to breast myopathies. Treat this as an exploratory biomarker problem.
- **Closest poultry evidence**: vocalization features have been used for **growth/body weight**, **thermal stress/comfort**, **distress/welfare**, and sometimes **respiratory/disease-like monitoring**. Recurrent feature families: **call rate / vocalization rate, duration, energy/amplitude, peak/dominant frequency, fundamental frequency, spectral distribution, MFCCs**.
- **General bioacoustics ML evidence**: public benchmark papers such as **BEANS** and **BirdSet** show modern bioacoustic classification generally benefits from spectrogram/CNN or SSL embeddings, but for **n=250 isolated hens with summary features**, classical tabular methods + rigorous validation are more appropriate.

---

## Ranked recipe table

### 1. Small-n tabular classifier with grouped/repeated CV + stability-based feature importance  
**Paper / evidence anchor**:  
- **BEANS: The Benchmark of Animal Sounds**, arXiv:2210.12300, 2022. Public bioacoustic ML benchmark.  
- Results read: Section 4 reports baselines across 12 animal-sound datasets; traditional models such as **logistic regression, SVM, decision trees, random forests**, and DL baselines were compared. Example reported scores include classification accuracies such as SVM ≈ **0.870** on Watkins aquatic mammals, **0.720** on bats, **0.779** on humpback, **0.914** on dogs; performance varied strongly by dataset.

**Dataset(s)**:  
- BEANS: 12 public animal-sound datasets, not poultry-specific. HF availability not verified.  
- Your dataset: 250 hens, one row/hen; tabular summary features. Format suitable.

**Method**:  
- Standardize continuous acoustic summaries.  
- Start with **elastic-net logistic regression**, **linear SVM**, **random forest / gradient boosting**, and **permutation importance**.  
- Use **repeated stratified CV**, or leave-batch/farm/recording-session-out if metadata exist.  
- For feature selection: use only inner-CV folds; avoid selecting features on the full dataset before CV.

**What made it work**:  
- For small bioacoustic datasets, simple models are competitive and easier to interpret.  
- Permutation importance on held-out folds guards against overfitting to noisy acoustic features.

**Overlapping features to prioritize**:  
- Vocalization/silence/event features: **vocalization rate, silence ratio, mean/SD event duration**.  
- Spectral: **dominant/peak frequency, spectral centroid, rolloff, bandwidth**.  
- Cepstral: **MFCC 1–13 or 1–20 means/SDs**, but reduce aggressively because MFCC stats are correlated.  
- F0: **f0 valid ratio**, f0 mean/SD/range.

---

### 2. Univariate screening + supervised multivariate model  
**Paper / evidence anchor**:  
- Scikit-learn feature-selection examples use ANOVA F-tests and SelectKBest for classification; not poultry-specific but appropriate for small-n tabular acoustic studies.  
- Farm-animal vocalization reviews generally report that pain/stress/welfare states often change **call rate, duration, amplitude/energy, and frequency structure**, making univariate tests interpretable.

**Result**:  
- No poultry-myopathy benchmark found.  
- Use as an interpretable hypothesis-generation recipe, not a final causal claim.

**Dataset(s)**:  
- Your 250-hen table. Requires binary label: myopathy present/absent or severity bin.

**Method**:  
- For each feature: compute effect size and p-value: Mann–Whitney or t-test; correct FDR.  
- Then train logistic regression / linear SVM on top-k stable features.  
- Recommended k grid: `k ∈ {5, 10, 15, 20}` inside nested CV.

**What made it work**:  
- Separates “which acoustic summaries differ?” from “which combination predicts?”  
- Reduces dimensionality before modeling when MFCC summaries dominate feature count.

**Likely significant feature families from poultry/welfare literature**:  
- **Higher call/vocalization rate** under distress/thermal discomfort.  
- **Longer or more intense calls** under stress/pain.  
- **Frequency shifts**: dominant/peak/fundamental frequency often changes with age, body size, and affective state.  
- **Spectral energy redistribution**: centroid/rolloff/bandwidth may reflect harsher/noisier calls or respiratory sounds.

---

### 3. PCA / supervised LDA / t-SNE or UMAP visualization for exploratory clusters  
**Paper / evidence anchor**:  
- Scikit-learn manifold example compares PCA/SVD, LDA, Isomap, LLE, MDS, t-SNE, NCA.  
- Bioacoustic benchmarks increasingly use learned embeddings, but for one-row-per-hen summary features, PCA/LDA/UMAP are more stable.

**Result**:  
- Visualization only; do **not** report t-SNE/UMAP cluster separation as performance.

**Dataset(s)**:  
- Your 250 rows. Suitable.

**Method**:  
- Standardize features.  
- PCA for global variance and loadings.  
- LDA or PLS-DA only inside CV if used predictively.  
- UMAP/t-SNE for visualization after PCA to ~10–30 components.

**What made it work**:  
- PCA loadings reveal redundant feature blocks: MFCC stats, spectral-shape stats, energy/event-rate stats.  
- Visualization helps detect outliers, recording artifacts, breed/age/batch confounding.

---

### 4. Bioacoustic deep/embedding models only if raw audio is available  
**Paper / evidence anchor**:  
- **BirdSet: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Classification in Avian Bioacoustics**, arXiv:2403.10380, 2024.  
- **BEANS**, arXiv:2210.12300, 2022.  
- These show modern bioacoustic classification uses mel-spectrograms/CNNs or pretrained encoders.

**Result**:  
- Not directly applicable to one-row-per-hen summary features unless raw waveforms are accessible.

**Dataset(s)**:  
- Requires raw audio clips, not just extracted features.

**Method**:  
- Use pretrained audio embeddings, then train shallow classifier.  
- With n=250, freeze encoder; do not fine-tune deep nets.

**What made it work**:  
- Learned embeddings can capture acoustic patterns beyond handcrafted summaries, but need careful validation and more audio.

---

## Feature-level findings relevant to your columns

### Strongest candidates
1. **Vocalization rate / silence ratio / event count**  
   - Welfare/stress literature repeatedly treats vocalization frequency as a distress/comfort indicator.  
   - For isolated hens, absence/presence of calls may itself be informative.

2. **Event duration statistics**  
   - Distress or pain calls in farm animals often differ in duration.  
   - Use mean, SD, median, IQR, and proportion of very short/long calls.

3. **Energy / RMS**  
   - Proxy for call intensity, but highly sensitive to microphone distance and gain.  
   - Normalize per recording/session if possible.

4. **Dominant frequency / spectral centroid / rolloff / bandwidth**  
   - Poultry growth/body-size acoustic work commonly focuses on dominant/peak frequency; spectral summaries overlap well with your centroid/rolloff/bandwidth features.

5. **F0 mean/SD/range + f0 valid ratio**  
   - F0 valid ratio may distinguish tonal calls from noisy/respiratory/low-quality audio.  
   - F0 estimates can be unreliable in chicken calls; include validity ratio and inspect distributions.

6. **MFCC 1–20 stats**  
   - Useful predictive features in general animal/bird bioacoustics, but numerous and correlated.  
   - Prefer grouped reduction: PCA on MFCC block or select stable MFCCs only.

### Lower confidence / caution
- **Zero-crossing rate**: can proxy noisiness/high-frequency energy but is sensitive to noise.  
- **Spectral flatness**: potentially useful for noisy/breathy calls but may capture recording noise.  
- **Raw RMS/energy**: risky unless gain/distance controlled.

---

## Code patterns from working examples

### Univariate feature selection

```python
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
from sklearn.svm import LinearSVC

selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=10)
clf = make_pipeline(
    selector,
    MinMaxScaler(),
    LinearSVC()
)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(clf.score(X_test, y_test))
selected = feature_names[clf[0].get_support()]
```

Source read: `scikit-learn/scikit-learn/examples/feature_selection/plot_feature_selection.py`

---

### Model-based selection and sequential selection

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel, SequentialFeatureSelector
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

base = LogisticRegression(
    penalty="elasticnet",
    solver="saga",
    l1_ratio=0.5,
    class_weight="balanced",
    max_iter=5000
)

sfm = SelectFromModel(base, threshold="median").fit(X_train, y_train)
X_train_sel = sfm.transform(X_train)

sfs = SequentialFeatureSelector(
    base,
    n_features_to_select=10,
    direction="forward"
).fit(X_train, y_train)
```

Source pattern read: `examples/feature_selection/plot_select_from_model_diabetes.py`

---

### Held-out permutation importance

```python
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance
import pandas as pd

result = permutation_importance(
    model, X_test, y_test,
    n_repeats=50,
    random_state=42,
    n_jobs=-1,
    scoring="roc_auc"
)

imp = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": feature_names,
    "mean_importance": result.importances_mean,
    "std_importance": result.importances_std,
}).sort_values("mean_importance", ascending=False)
```

Source read: `scikit-learn/scikit-learn/examples/inspection/plot_permutation_importance.py`

---

### Visualization embeddings

```python
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.discriminant_analysis import LinearDiscriminantAnalysis
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE

Xz = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)

pca2 = PCA(n_components=2, random_state=0).fit_transform(Xz)
lda1 = LinearDiscriminantAnalysis(n_components=1).fit_transform(Xz, y)

tsne2 = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    max_iter=500,
    n_iter_without_progress=150,
    random_state=0,
    init="pca"
).fit_transform(Xz)
```

Source pattern read: `scikit-learn/scikit-learn/examples/manifold/plot_lle_digits.py`

---

## Recommendations

1. **Implement first**: nested/repeated CV with elastic-net logistic regression + random forest/gradient boosting, reporting ROC-AUC, balanced accuracy, sensitivity/specificity, and calibration.  
2. **Feature blocks to test**:
   - Event/rate block: silence ratio, vocalization rate, event duration stats.
   - Energy block: RMS/energy stats.
   - Spectral block: centroid, bandwidth, rolloff, flatness, ZCR.
   - MFCC block: MFCC 1–20 mean/SD; reduce by PCA or stability selection.
   - F0 block: f0 mean/SD/min/max/range/valid ratio.
3. **Dimensionality reduction**:
   - Use PCA loadings for interpretation.
   - Use UMAP/t-SNE only for plots.
   - Use LDA/PLS only inside CV if predictive.
4. **Most important statistical guardrail**: with only 250 hens, all preprocessing/feature selection must be fit **inside the training fold**.  
5. **Literature gap**: no verified evidence that hen vocalizations directly indicate woody breast/white striping. Frame findings as exploratory predictors of health/stress/body condition, not established myopathy biomarkers.

messages.content:
Below is a practical overview tailored to your dataset: **250 hens × ~170 acoustic summary features**, with a binary outcome: **myopathy present/absent**.

Key point first: I found **no strong direct literature showing that hen/chicken vocalizations are already validated biomarkers of muscular myopathies** such as woody breast, white striping, spaghetti meat, etc. Existing poultry/bioacoustics literature is much stronger for **welfare, stress, thermal comfort, growth/body weight, distress calls, respiratory monitoring, and general animal-sound classification**. So your problem should be framed as an **exploratory biomarker/predictive modelling study**, not as a replication of an established myopathy-vocalization marker.

---

# 1. Your dataset structure and main statistical issue

You have approximately:

- **n = 250 hens**
- **p ≈ 170 acoustic features**
- Binary label: myopathy presence/absence

This is a **small-n, moderate-p** tabular problem. The danger is not that you lack features; the danger is that you have **too many correlated acoustic summaries** relative to the number of hens.

Most features are likely highly correlated:

- `mfcc_01_mean`, `mfcc_01_median`, `mfcc_01_p10`, `mfcc_01_p90`, etc.
- Spectral centroid / bandwidth / rolloff are often correlated.
- RMS features correlate with recording distance/gain.
- F0 features may be unreliable when calls are noisy or unvoiced.

So the central goal should be:

> Identify a small, stable, interpretable set of acoustic variables or low-dimensional components that separate myopathic vs non-myopathic hens, while avoiding overfitting.

---

# 2. Pre-analysis checks before feature selection

Before any feature selection, I strongly recommend these checks.

## 2.1 Remove non-informative identifiers

Do **not** use:

```text
chicken_id
```

as a model feature.

It is an identifier, not a biological predictor. Keep it only for traceability.

## 2.2 Check label distribution

You need to know:

```text
myopathy present: n = ?
myopathy absent: n = ?
```

If the classes are imbalanced, e.g. 40 diseased vs 210 healthy, accuracy becomes misleading. Prefer:

- ROC-AUC
- PR-AUC
- balanced accuracy
- sensitivity/specificity
- F1
- MCC
- calibrated probability curves

## 2.3 Inspect missing values

Especially important for:

```text
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_min
f0_max
f0_valid_ratio
```

F0 extraction can fail if the signal is noisy, broadband, or unvoiced. Missing or unreliable F0 values may themselves be informative, but you should distinguish:

- true biological absence of tonal vocalization
- extraction failure
- low-quality recording

## 2.4 Standardize all numeric features

Most methods require scaling:

```text
z = (x - mean) / std
```

This is essential for:

- PCA
- logistic regression
- SVM
- LASSO / elastic net
- kNN
- PLS-DA
- t-SNE / UMAP

Tree-based models technically do not require it, but for a unified workflow it is still useful.

## 2.5 Detect outliers

Audio features are often dominated by recording artefacts.

Check:

- extreme `duration_sec`
- extreme `rms_max`
- very high `silence_percent`
- zero or near-zero `vocalization_rate_per_min`
- suspicious `sample_rate` differences
- extreme spectral centroid/rolloff
- impossible F0 values

Use:

- boxplots
- robust z-scores
- PCA outlier plots
- Mahalanobis distance
- isolation forest only for diagnostic purposes

---

# 3. Feature families in your dataset

Your features can be grouped into biologically meaningful blocks.

## 3.1 Recording-level/control features

```text
duration_sec
sample_rate
```

These should usually not be interpreted biologically.

- `duration_sec`: may affect number of detected events.
- `sample_rate`: should ideally be constant. If not, it can introduce artefacts.

Use them for quality control, not necessarily as predictors.

---

## 3.2 Energy/intensity features

```text
rms_mean
rms_std
rms_max
num_high_energy_events
```

Potential interpretation:

- louder or more forceful vocalizations
- agitation/distress
- microphone distance/gain artefact
- movement or environmental noise

Caution: RMS is highly sensitive to recording setup. If microphone distance and gain were not standardized, energy features may reflect technical variation.

---

## 3.3 Temporal/vocal activity features

```text
silence_percent
num_high_energy_events
vocalization_rate_per_min
mean_event_duration_sec
duration_sec
```

These are among the most promising for your goal because they summarize behavioural activity.

Potential interpretation:

- sick or impaired hens may vocalize less/more
- isolation distress may differ by health state
- myopathic birds may show altered activity or stress responses

Important features:

```text
silence_percent
vocalization_rate_per_min
mean_event_duration_sec
num_high_energy_events
```

These should be among your first candidates.

---

## 3.4 Spectral-shape features

```text
zcr_mean
zcr_std
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_centroid_std
spectral_bandwidth_mean
spectral_bandwidth_std
spectral_rolloff_mean
spectral_rolloff_std
spectral_flatness_mean
spectral_flatness_std
```

Potential interpretation:

- higher centroid/rolloff: more high-frequency energy
- higher bandwidth: broader/noisier calls
- higher flatness: more noise-like signal
- ZCR: rough high-frequency/noisiness proxy

These could reflect:

- harshness of calls
- respiratory/noisy sounds
- stress-related vocal changes
- recording noise

Promising features:

```text
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_rolloff_mean
spectral_bandwidth_mean
spectral_flatness_mean
zcr_mean
```

Their standard deviations may also be useful because variability across the recording can reflect irregular calling patterns.

---

## 3.5 MFCC features

You have for each MFCC 1–20:

```text
mean
std
min
max
median
p10
p90
```

This is the largest block of features.

MFCCs summarize spectral envelope/timbre. They are widely used in:

- speech analysis
- animal vocalization classification
- bird/bioacoustic classification
- sound-event recognition

But in your case they create a high-dimensional correlated block:

```text
20 MFCCs × 7 statistics = 140 features
```

This block alone is more than half your dataset size. You should **not** interpret all MFCC features individually without reduction or stability analysis.

Recommended handling:

1. Use only MFCC means and standard deviations initially.
2. Or apply PCA only to the MFCC block.
3. Or use elastic-net/LASSO stability selection.
4. Or group them as a feature block and compare model performance with/without MFCCs.

I would avoid starting with all 140 MFCC-derived variables in an unregularized model.

---

## 3.6 Fundamental frequency/pitch features

```text
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_min
f0_max
f0_valid_ratio
```

Potential interpretation:

- pitch shifts
- tonal vs noisy calls
- physiological/vocal tract differences
- stress-related changes
- call-type composition

Most important:

```text
f0_valid_ratio
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_max - f0_min
```

I would create an additional derived feature:

```text
f0_range = f0_max - f0_min
```

Caution: Chicken vocalizations can be noisy, and F0 estimation may be unstable. Always examine `f0_valid_ratio`.

---

# 4. Dimensionality reduction strategies

Dimensionality reduction methods transform many features into fewer variables. Some are useful for interpretation; others only for visualization.

---

## 4.1 PCA: Principal Component Analysis

### What it does

PCA finds linear combinations of features that explain maximum variance.

### Useful for your dataset?

Yes. PCA is a good first method because your features are highly correlated.

### How to use it

Use PCA after standardization:

```text
StandardScaler → PCA
```

You can apply PCA to:

1. all numeric acoustic features
2. only the MFCC block
3. only spectral features
4. only temporal/energy features

### Recommended use

For your dataset, I would use **block-wise PCA**:

- PCA on MFCC features
- PCA on spectral features
- PCA on energy/event features
- PCA on F0 features if enough valid F0 data exist

Then use the first few PCs from each block in a classifier.

Example:

```text
MFCC_PC1, MFCC_PC2, MFCC_PC3
Spectral_PC1, Spectral_PC2
Energy_PC1
F0_PC1
```

This reduces ~170 variables to perhaps 8–15 components.

### Pros

- Handles correlated features.
- Reduces overfitting.
- Gives loadings showing which variables drive each component.
- Works well with small datasets.

### Cons

- Unsupervised: components maximize variance, not disease separation.
- PCs can be harder to interpret biologically.

### Best use

Use PCA for:

- exploratory analysis
- reducing MFCC block
- detecting outliers
- creating compact predictors

---

## 4.2 Sparse PCA

Sparse PCA is like PCA but forces many feature loadings to zero.

### Useful for your case?

Potentially yes, because it creates components that depend on fewer features.

### Pros

- More interpretable than standard PCA.
- Useful when features are highly redundant.

### Cons

- Requires tuning sparsity.
- Can be unstable with n = 250.
- Less standard than PCA.

Use it after simpler PCA and regularized models.

---

## 4.3 PLS-DA: Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis

PLS-DA is supervised dimensionality reduction. It finds components that maximize covariance between features and class labels.

### Useful for your case?

Yes, but with caution.

PLS-DA is common in omics/biomarker-style datasets where p is high and n is moderate. Your dataset is similar in structure.

### Pros

- Supervised: components are optimized for myopathy separation.
- Handles correlated predictors.
- Can rank feature importance through VIP scores.

### Cons

- Easy to overfit.
- Must be tuned inside cross-validation.
- PLS-DA plots can look overly optimistic if fitted on the full dataset.

### Recommendation

Use PLS-DA only with:

- repeated cross-validation
- nested tuning of number of components
- permutation testing

Do **not** fit PLS-DA once on the whole dataset and claim separation from the resulting plot.

---

## 4.4 LDA: Linear Discriminant Analysis

LDA finds a linear axis separating two groups.

### Useful?

For visualization, yes. For prediction, maybe.

With binary labels, LDA gives one discriminant axis.

### Pros

- Simple and interpretable.
- Good for visualizing class separation.

### Cons

- Assumes approximately Gaussian classes with similar covariance.
- With many correlated features, can be unstable.
- Needs dimensionality reduction first if p is high.

### Best workflow

```text
StandardScaler → PCA → LDA
```

Example:

```text
Use first 5–20 PCs, then LDA.
```

---

## 4.5 UMAP

UMAP is a nonlinear visualization method.

### Useful?

Yes, for exploratory visualization.

### Pros

- Can reveal nonlinear clustering.
- Often better than t-SNE for preserving some global structure.
- Good for visualizing whether myopathic/non-myopathic hens form clusters.

### Cons

- Not a statistical test.
- Sensitive to parameters.
- Can show apparent clusters even in weak data.
- Should not be used as proof of separability.

### Recommended use

Use:

```text
StandardScaler → PCA to 10–30 dimensions → UMAP to 2D
```

Color points by:

- myopathy present/absent
- severity score if available
- batch/farm/day
- body weight
- age
- recording duration
- microphone/session

This helps detect confounding.

---

## 4.6 t-SNE

t-SNE is another nonlinear visualization method.

### Useful?

Only for visualization.

### Pros

- Good at showing local neighborhoods.
- Can reveal subgroups.

### Cons

- Very sensitive to perplexity and random seed.
- Poor global distance interpretation.
- Not suitable for feature selection.
- Can produce misleading clusters.

### Recommendation

Use t-SNE only after PCA and only as a supplementary plot.

---

## 4.7 Autoencoders

With only 250 hens, I would not recommend training a neural autoencoder on these tabular features.

### Why?

- Too little data.
- High overfitting risk.
- Hard to interpret.
- PCA will likely be more stable.

Autoencoders may become useful only if you return to the raw audio and have many segmented calls.

---

# 5. Feature selection strategies

Feature selection keeps original features rather than transforming them.

This is important because you likely want interpretable acoustic biomarkers.

---

## 5.1 Filter methods: simple univariate screening

These methods test each feature individually against the label.

### Options

For each feature, compare myopathic vs non-myopathic hens using:

- t-test, if approximately normal
- Mann–Whitney U test, if non-normal
- point-biserial correlation
- ROC-AUC per feature
- mutual information
- Cohen’s d or Cliff’s delta effect size

### Recommended outputs

For each feature:

```text
feature
mean/median in healthy
mean/median in myopathic
effect size
p-value
FDR-adjusted p-value
single-feature ROC-AUC
```

### Pros

- Very interpretable.
- Good first screening.
- Helps identify candidate biomarkers.

### Cons

- Ignores interactions between features.
- Correlated features create redundancy.
- Multiple testing problem.

### Important

You have ~170 features. Apply correction:

```text
Benjamini-Hochberg FDR
```

not only raw p-values.

### Features to prioritize in univariate analysis

Start with:

```text
silence_percent
vocalization_rate_per_min
num_high_energy_events
mean_event_duration_sec
rms_mean
rms_std
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_bandwidth_mean
spectral_rolloff_mean
spectral_flatness_mean
zcr_mean
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_valid_ratio
```

Then examine MFCC features.

---

## 5.2 Correlation-based pruning

Before model-based selection, remove redundant features.

### Method

Compute Spearman or Pearson correlation between all features.

If two features have:

```text
|r| > 0.85 or |r| > 0.90
```

keep only one.

For example, these may be redundant:

```text
mfcc_01_mean
mfcc_01_median
mfcc_01_p10
mfcc_01_p90
```

### How to choose which one to keep

Prefer features that are:

1. more interpretable
2. less missing
3. more stable
4. stronger univariate association with label
5. biologically plausible

### Pros

- Reduces redundancy.
- Makes models more stable.
- Helps interpretation.

### Cons

- Does not consider multivariate interactions.

---

## 5.3 Variance thresholding

Remove features with near-zero variance.

Examples:

- constant `sample_rate`
- features with almost identical values across hens
- F0 features if most calls failed

This should be done before more advanced methods.

---

## 5.4 LASSO logistic regression

LASSO logistic regression uses L1 regularization to shrink some coefficients to zero.

### Useful?

Very useful for your dataset.

### Model

```text
myopathy ~ selected acoustic features
```

with L1 penalty.

### Pros

- Performs classification and feature selection simultaneously.
- Good for high-dimensional correlated data.
- Produces a small feature set.

### Cons

- When features are highly correlated, LASSO may arbitrarily choose one of several equivalent features.
- Selected features can vary across folds.

### Recommendation

Use **stability selection**:

1. Run LASSO inside repeated cross-validation.
2. Record which features are selected.
3. Keep features selected in, e.g., >60–80% of folds.

This gives more reliable candidates.

---

## 5.5 Elastic-net logistic regression

Elastic net combines L1 and L2 penalties.

### Useful?

Probably one of the best first models for your dataset.

### Why?

Your features are correlated. Elastic net is more stable than pure LASSO.

### Tune

```text
C or lambda
l1_ratio
```

Example l1 ratios:

```text
0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9
```

### Pros

- Handles correlated predictors better than LASSO.
- Gives interpretable coefficients.
- Works well with small tabular datasets.
- Can include class weights for imbalance.

### Cons

- Still linear.
- Needs careful cross-validation.

---

## 5.6 Recursive Feature Elimination, RFE

RFE repeatedly fits a model and removes weak features.

### Common base models

- logistic regression
- linear SVM
- random forest
- gradient boosting

### Useful?

Yes, but computationally heavier and potentially unstable.

### Recommendation

Use RFE only after reducing obvious redundancy.

Example:

```text
correlation pruning → RFE with logistic regression/SVM → final model
```

### Pros

- Multivariate.
- Can find compact feature sets.

### Cons

- Can overfit if not nested inside CV.
- Feature rankings can be unstable with correlated variables.

---

## 5.7 Sequential feature selection

Forward selection:

1. Start with zero features.
2. Add the feature that improves CV score most.
3. Continue until performance stops improving.

Backward selection:

1. Start with all features.
2. Remove weakest features.

### Useful?

Maybe, but with caution.

For n = 250 and ~170 features, forward selection may be feasible after correlation pruning.

### Pros

- Directly optimizes model performance.
- Can produce small feature sets.

### Cons

- Greedy.
- Can overfit.
- Computationally expensive.
- Must be nested inside cross-validation.

---

## 5.8 Tree-based feature importance

Models:

- random forest
- extremely randomized trees
- gradient boosting
- XGBoost / LightGBM / CatBoost

### Useful?

Yes, especially for nonlinear relationships.

### Feature importance types

Avoid relying only on default Gini importance. Prefer:

```text
permutation importance
SHAP values
```

### Pros

- Captures nonlinear effects.
- Captures interactions.
- Handles mixed feature scales.

### Cons

- Can overfit with n = 250.
- Importance is unstable with correlated predictors.
- Default importance can be biased toward continuous/noisy features.

### Recommendation

Use tree models as a complementary approach, not the only method.

A good workflow:

```text
Random forest / gradient boosting → repeated CV → permutation importance on held-out folds
```

---

## 5.9 Permutation importance

Permutation importance measures how much model performance drops when one feature is randomly shuffled.

### Useful?

Very useful.

### Pros

- Model-agnostic.
- Evaluates importance based on predictive contribution.
- Can be computed on held-out folds.

### Cons

- Correlated features can hide each other’s importance.
- If two features contain the same information, permuting one may not reduce performance much.

### Recommendation

Use **grouped permutation importance**.

For example, permute whole feature groups:

```text
Energy block
Temporal/event block
Spectral block
MFCC block
F0 block
```

This may be more informative than single-feature importance.

---

## 5.10 SHAP analysis

SHAP can explain predictions from tree models or linear models.

### Useful?

Yes, after you have a reasonably validated model.

### Pros

- Shows directionality.
- Can reveal nonlinear thresholds.
- Useful for interpretation.

Example interpretation:

```text
High silence_percent increases predicted probability of myopathy.
Low f0_valid_ratio increases predicted probability of myopathy.
High spectral_flatness_mean increases predicted probability of myopathy.
```

### Cons

- Can be unstable in small datasets.
- Correlated features complicate interpretation.
- Should not replace statistical validation.

---

## 5.11 Stability selection

This is one of the most important strategies for your case.

### Idea

A feature is credible if it is repeatedly selected across many resampled datasets.

### Procedure

1. Repeat cross-validation many times.
2. Fit feature selection only on training folds.
3. Record selected features.
4. Rank by selection frequency.

Example output:

| Feature | Selection frequency |
|---|---:|
| vocalization_rate_per_min | 0.86 |
| spectral_flatness_mean | 0.78 |
| f0_valid_ratio | 0.74 |
| mfcc_03_std | 0.67 |
| silence_percent | 0.64 |

### Pros

- Reduces false discoveries.
- Handles small sample size better.
- Produces more trustworthy feature rankings.

### Cons

- Requires repeated modelling.
- Results depend on model family.

### Recommendation

Use stability selection with:

- elastic-net logistic regression
- linear SVM
- random forest/permutation importance

Then look for agreement across methods.

---

# 6. Visualization techniques

Visualization is essential, but it should not be confused with proof.

---

## 6.1 Feature distribution plots

For each candidate feature, plot:

- histogram/density by class
- boxplot
- violin plot
- swarm/strip plot

Best for:

```text
vocalization_rate_per_min
silence_percent
mean_event_duration_sec
rms_mean
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_flatness_mean
f0_valid_ratio
f0_mean
```

These plots help identify:

- group differences
- outliers
- skewness
- threshold-like effects

---

## 6.2 Correlation heatmap

Make a correlation heatmap of all features or feature blocks.

Recommended:

- Spearman correlation
- hierarchical clustering of features

This will reveal redundancy among:

- MFCC statistics
- spectral features
- energy features

Useful for deciding which features to prune.

---

## 6.3 PCA score plot

Plot PC1 vs PC2, colored by:

- myopathy status
- batch
- recording session
- breed/line
- age
- body weight
- lesion score if available

Use this to answer:

- Are myopathic hens globally separated?
- Are there recording artefacts?
- Are there batch effects?
- Are outliers driving the result?

---

## 6.4 PCA loading plot

A PCA loading plot tells you which features contribute most to each principal component.

Example:

- PC1 may represent general vocal activity/energy.
- PC2 may represent spectral brightness/noisiness.
- PC3 may represent MFCC/timbre variation.

This helps convert abstract components into biological interpretations.

---

## 6.5 Volcano plot

For univariate analysis:

x-axis:

```text
effect size
```

y-axis:

```text
-log10(FDR-adjusted p-value)
```

This identifies features that are both statistically associated and biologically meaningful.

---

## 6.6 ROC curves for top single features

For each top feature, compute single-feature ROC-AUC.

Example:

```text
silence_percent alone: AUC = ?
vocalization_rate_per_min alone: AUC = ?
f0_valid_ratio alone: AUC = ?
spectral_flatness_mean alone: AUC = ?
```

This helps assess whether any one feature is informative.

---

## 6.7 UMAP/t-SNE plots

Use only as exploratory visualization.

Recommended pipeline:

```text
StandardScaler → PCA to 10–30 PCs → UMAP/t-SNE to 2D
```

Color by:

- myopathy status
- severity
- batch
- body weight
- age
- recording quality

If clusters align with batch rather than disease, that signals confounding.

---

## 6.8 SHAP summary plot

After fitting a validated tree-based model, use SHAP summary plots to show:

- most influential features
- whether high/low values increase risk
- nonlinear patterns

This can be very useful for communicating results.

---

# 7. Recommended modelling/selection workflow

For your dataset, I would use this pipeline.

---

## Step 1: Quality control

Remove:

```text
chicken_id
```

Check:

```text
duration_sec
sample_rate
missing values
outliers
f0_valid_ratio
class balance
```

Create derived features:

```text
f0_range = f0_max - f0_min
rms_range = rms_max - rms_mean
```

Possibly also:

```text
mfcc_i_range = mfcc_i_p90 - mfcc_i_p10
```

for each MFCC, but only if you later reduce dimensionality.

---

## Step 2: Define feature blocks

Use these blocks:

### Temporal/event block

```text
silence_percent
num_high_energy_events
vocalization_rate_per_min
mean_event_duration_sec
duration_sec
```

### Energy block

```text
rms_mean
rms_std
rms_max
```

### Spectral block

```text
zcr_mean
zcr_std
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_centroid_std
spectral_bandwidth_mean
spectral_bandwidth_std
spectral_rolloff_mean
spectral_rolloff_std
spectral_flatness_mean
spectral_flatness_std
```

### F0 block

```text
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_min
f0_max
f0_valid_ratio
f0_range
```

### MFCC block

Either all MFCC features or a reduced subset.

I would initially use:

```text
mfcc_01_mean ... mfcc_20_mean
mfcc_01_std ... mfcc_20_std
```

Then add min/max/median/p10/p90 only if justified.

---

## Step 3: Univariate screening

For each feature:

- compute group medians
- compute effect size
- compute p-value
- apply FDR correction
- compute single-feature ROC-AUC

This gives interpretable candidate biomarkers.

---

## Step 4: Correlation pruning

Within highly correlated feature clusters, keep only one representative.

Threshold:

```text
|Spearman r| > 0.85
```

or stricter:

```text
|Spearman r| > 0.90
```

---

## Step 5: Fit regularized models

Use repeated stratified cross-validation.

Models:

1. elastic-net logistic regression
2. LASSO logistic regression
3. linear SVM
4. random forest
5. gradient boosting

Metrics:

- ROC-AUC
- PR-AUC
- balanced accuracy
- sensitivity
- specificity
- MCC

---

## Step 6: Stability selection

For each repeated CV fold, record selected/important features.

Rank features by:

```text
selection frequency
mean absolute coefficient
permutation importance
SHAP importance
```

Features repeatedly selected across methods are your strongest candidates.

---

## Step 7: Block-level modelling

Compare models using different feature groups:

| Model | Features |
|---|---|
| A | Temporal/event only |
| B | Energy only |
| C | Spectral only |
| D | F0 only |
| E | MFCC only |
| F | Temporal + spectral |
| G | Temporal + spectral + F0 |
| H | All features |
| I | PCA-reduced all features |

This tells you whether myopathy information is mostly in:

- vocal activity
- spectral quality
- pitch
- MFCC timbre
- energy

This is more biologically interpretable than throwing all features into one model.

---

# 8. Recommended validation strategy

Because n = 250, validation is critical.

## 8.1 Use nested cross-validation

Feature selection must be inside the training fold.

Bad:

```text
Select top 20 features on all data → cross-validate model
```

This leaks label information and gives inflated performance.

Good:

```text
Outer CV:
    split train/test
    Inner CV:
        select features
        tune model
    Evaluate once on outer test fold
```

## 8.2 Use repeated stratified CV

Recommended:

```text
5-fold CV × 20 repeats
```

or:

```text
10-fold CV × 10 repeats
```

If class imbalance is strong, use stratification.

## 8.3 If you have batch/farm/recording-day metadata

Use grouped validation:

```text
GroupKFold
Leave-one-batch-out
Leave-one-farm-out
```

This is extremely important if recordings were collected in batches.

Otherwise, your model may learn recording/session artefacts instead of myopathy biology.

---

# 9. Literature findings relevant to your scope

## 9.1 Direct myopathy-vocalization literature

I found **no clear existing literature directly validating vocal acoustic features as biomarkers of poultry muscular myopathies**.

That means there is likely no established feature such as:

```text
MFCC_07_std is known to indicate woody breast
```

or:

```text
f0_mean is a validated biomarker of white striping
```

So your work is exploratory and potentially novel.

---

## 9.2 Related poultry vocalization literature

The closest related areas are:

### Welfare/stress monitoring

Poultry vocalizations are commonly studied as indicators of:

- stress
- fear
- isolation distress
- thermal discomfort
- general welfare

Commonly relevant acoustic variables:

```text
call rate
call duration
amplitude/energy
dominant frequency
fundamental frequency
spectral distribution
```

These overlap with your features:

```text
vocalization_rate_per_min
mean_event_duration_sec
rms_mean
rms_std
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_rolloff_mean
f0_mean
f0_std
```

---

### Growth/body condition monitoring

Some poultry sound studies use flock vocalization features to estimate growth/body weight or age-related changes.

Relevant acoustic dimensions:

```text
dominant frequency
spectral distribution
call intensity
temporal activity
```

This matters because myopathies in broilers are often associated with growth rate, body composition, or metabolic stress. If myopathic hens also differ in body size or condition, acoustic differences may be indirect.

Potential confounders:

```text
body weight
age
breed/line
recording day
farm
environment
microphone distance
```

---

### Respiratory/disease monitoring

Sound analysis has been explored for detecting abnormal respiratory sounds or health status in poultry and livestock.

Relevant variables:

```text
spectral flatness
spectral bandwidth
spectral centroid
ZCR
MFCCs
energy distribution
```

These overlap with your:

```text
spectral_flatness_mean
spectral_bandwidth_mean
spectral_centroid_mean
zcr_mean
mfcc_* features
```

But respiratory disease is not the same as muscular myopathy. Interpretation should be cautious.

---

## 9.3 General bioacoustics literature

Broader animal-sound classification work supports the use of:

- MFCCs
- spectral features
- energy features
- temporal call features
- pretrained audio embeddings if raw audio is available

Relevant benchmark papers:

- **BEANS: The Benchmark of Animal Sounds**  
  https://huggingface.co/papers/2210.12300

- **BirdSet: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Classification in Avian Bioacoustics**  
  https://huggingface.co/papers/2403.10380

These are not myopathy-specific, but they support the general idea that acoustic features can encode biologically relevant information.

---

# 10. Features I would prioritize biologically

Given your exact feature list, I would initially focus on these.

## Highest-priority candidates

```text
silence_percent
vocalization_rate_per_min
num_high_energy_events
mean_event_duration_sec
rms_mean
rms_std
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_bandwidth_mean
spectral_rolloff_mean
spectral_flatness_mean
zcr_mean
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_valid_ratio
```

Why these?

They are interpretable and connected to plausible behavioural/physiological mechanisms:

- amount of calling
- intensity of calling
- temporal structure
- harshness/noisiness
- pitch
- tonal reliability

---

## Medium-priority candidates

```text
spectral_centroid_std
spectral_bandwidth_std
spectral_rolloff_std
spectral_flatness_std
zcr_std
f0_min
f0_max
```

These measure variability, which may reflect unstable or heterogeneous vocal behavior.

---

## MFCC candidates

MFCCs may be predictive, but I would not manually inspect 140 MFCC statistics independently.

I would use one of these approaches:

### Option A: reduce MFCCs

Use only:

```text
mfcc_01_mean ... mfcc_20_mean
mfcc_01_std ... mfcc_20_std
```

### Option B: PCA on MFCC block

Create:

```text
MFCC_PC1
MFCC_PC2
MFCC_PC3
MFCC_PC4
MFCC_PC5
```

### Option C: stability selection

Let elastic net select stable MFCCs across folds.

---

# 11. What results would be convincing?

A convincing result would not be:

> A t-SNE plot shows two clusters.

A convincing result would be:

1. Repeated/nested cross-validation shows performance above chance.
2. Permutation test confirms the model is not exploiting random label structure.
3. Feature selection is stable.
4. Top features are biologically plausible.
5. Results survive adjustment for confounders such as body weight, age, batch, or recording conditions.
6. Similar feature families are selected across different model types.

For example, a credible conclusion would look like:

> Across repeated nested cross-validation, temporal and spectral features predicted myopathy status with ROC-AUC 0.74 ± 0.08. The most stable features were `vocalization_rate_per_min`, `silence_percent`, `spectral_flatness_mean`, and `f0_valid_ratio`. MFCC features improved performance modestly but were less stable. These results suggest that myopathic hens may differ in vocal activity and call noisiness, although external validation is required.

---

# 12. Recommended final analysis plan

If I were analyzing your data, I would do this:

## Phase 1: Exploratory analysis

- Missing values
- Outliers
- Class balance
- Correlation heatmap
- PCA plot
- Distribution plots of key features

## Phase 2: Univariate statistics

For each feature:

- group median/mean
- effect size
- p-value
- FDR-adjusted p-value
- single-feature AUC

## Phase 3: Feature-block analysis

Compare predictive value of:

1. temporal/event features
2. energy features
3. spectral features
4. F0 features
5. MFCC features
6. all features
7. PCA-reduced features

## Phase 4: Regularized modelling

Use:

- elastic-net logistic regression
- linear SVM
- random forest or gradient boosting

With:

- repeated stratified nested CV
- feature selection inside folds
- class weighting if imbalanced

## Phase 5: Interpretation

Use:

- stability selection
- permutation importance
- SHAP
- PCA loadings
- effect-size plots

## Phase 6: Sensitivity analysis

Repeat after controlling for:

- duration
- sample rate
- body weight
- age
- batch
- recording day
- microphone/session

if available.

---

# 13. Bottom-line recommendation

For your specific problem, the strongest strategy is:

> Combine interpretable univariate screening, correlation pruning, elastic-net stability selection, and block-wise PCA/MFCC reduction, all evaluated inside repeated nested cross-validation.

The most promising feature families are likely:

1. **Vocal activity**
   ```text
   vocalization_rate_per_min
   silence_percent
   num_high_energy_events
   mean_event_duration_sec
   ```

2. **Spectral/noisiness features**
   ```text
   spectral_centroid_mean
   spectral_bandwidth_mean
   spectral_rolloff_mean
   spectral_flatness_mean
   zcr_mean
   ```

3. **Pitch/F0 reliability**
   ```text
   f0_mean
   f0_std
   f0_valid_ratio
   ```

4. **Reduced MFCC/timbre features**
   ```text
   MFCC PCA components or stable MFCC coefficients
   ```

The most important caution is that **there is no established direct myopathy-vocalization biomarker literature**, so the study should be presented as exploratory and validated carefully.

data.output:
## SOTA / evidence landscape

- **Direct hen/chicken vocalization → myopathies (woody breast / white striping)**: I found **no direct acoustic-vocalization papers** linking hen/broiler calls to breast myopathies. Treat this as an exploratory biomarker problem.
- **Closest poultry evidence**: vocalization features have been used for **growth/body weight**, **thermal stress/comfort**, **distress/welfare**, and sometimes **respiratory/disease-like monitoring**. Recurrent feature families: **call rate / vocalization rate, duration, energy/amplitude, peak/dominant frequency, fundamental frequency, spectral distribution, MFCCs**.
- **General bioacoustics ML evidence**: public benchmark papers such as **BEANS** and **BirdSet** show modern bioacoustic classification generally benefits from spectrogram/CNN or SSL embeddings, but for **n=250 isolated hens with summary features**, classical tabular methods + rigorous validation are more appropriate.

---

## Ranked recipe table

### 1. Small-n tabular classifier with grouped/repeated CV + stability-based feature importance  
**Paper / evidence anchor**:  
- **BEANS: The Benchmark of Animal Sounds**, arXiv:2210.12300, 2022. Public bioacoustic ML benchmark.  
- Results read: Section 4 reports baselines across 12 animal-sound datasets; traditional models such as **logistic regression, SVM, decision trees, random forests**, and DL baselines were compared. Example reported scores include classification accuracies such as SVM ≈ **0.870** on Watkins aquatic mammals, **0.720** on bats, **0.779** on humpback, **0.914** on dogs; performance varied strongly by dataset.

**Dataset(s)**:  
- BEANS: 12 public animal-sound datasets, not poultry-specific. HF availability not verified.  
- Your dataset: 250 hens, one row/hen; tabular summary features. Format suitable.

**Method**:  
- Standardize continuous acoustic summaries.  
- Start with **elastic-net logistic regression**, **linear SVM**, **random forest / gradient boosting**, and **permutation importance**.  
- Use **repeated stratified CV**, or leave-batch/farm/recording-session-out if metadata exist.  
- For feature selection: use only inner-CV folds; avoid selecting features on the full dataset before CV.

**What made it work**:  
- For small bioacoustic datasets, simple models are competitive and easier to interpret.  
- Permutation importance on held-out folds guards against overfitting to noisy acoustic features.

**Overlapping features to prioritize**:  
- Vocalization/silence/event features: **vocalization rate, silence ratio, mean/SD event duration**.  
- Spectral: **dominant/peak frequency, spectral centroid, rolloff, bandwidth**.  
- Cepstral: **MFCC 1–13 or 1–20 means/SDs**, but reduce aggressively because MFCC stats are correlated.  
- F0: **f0 valid ratio**, f0 mean/SD/range.

---

### 2. Univariate screening + supervised multivariate model  
**Paper / evidence anchor**:  
- Scikit-learn feature-selection examples use ANOVA F-tests and SelectKBest for classification; not poultry-specific but appropriate for small-n tabular acoustic studies.  
- Farm-animal vocalization reviews generally report that pain/stress/welfare states often change **call rate, duration, amplitude/energy, and frequency structure**, making univariate tests interpretable.

**Result**:  
- No poultry-myopathy benchmark found.  
- Use as an interpretable hypothesis-generation recipe, not a final causal claim.

**Dataset(s)**:  
- Your 250-hen table. Requires binary label: myopathy present/absent or severity bin.

**Method**:  
- For each feature: compute effect size and p-value: Mann–Whitney or t-test; correct FDR.  
- Then train logistic regression / linear SVM on top-k stable features.  
- Recommended k grid: `k ∈ {5, 10, 15, 20}` inside nested CV.

**What made it work**:  
- Separates “which acoustic summaries differ?” from “which combination predicts?”  
- Reduces dimensionality before modeling when MFCC summaries dominate feature count.

**Likely significant feature families from poultry/welfare literature**:  
- **Higher call/vocalization rate** under distress/thermal discomfort.  
- **Longer or more intense calls** under stress/pain.  
- **Frequency shifts**: dominant/peak/fundamental frequency often changes with age, body size, and affective state.  
- **Spectral energy redistribution**: centroid/rolloff/bandwidth may reflect harsher/noisier calls or respiratory sounds.

---

### 3. PCA / supervised LDA / t-SNE or UMAP visualization for exploratory clusters  
**Paper / evidence anchor**:  
- Scikit-learn manifold example compares PCA/SVD, LDA, Isomap, LLE, MDS, t-SNE, NCA.  
- Bioacoustic benchmarks increasingly use learned embeddings, but for one-row-per-hen summary features, PCA/LDA/UMAP are more stable.

**Result**:  
- Visualization only; do **not** report t-SNE/UMAP cluster separation as performance.

**Dataset(s)**:  
- Your 250 rows. Suitable.

**Method**:  
- Standardize features.  
- PCA for global variance and loadings.  
- LDA or PLS-DA only inside CV if used predictively.  
- UMAP/t-SNE for visualization after PCA to ~10–30 components.

**What made it work**:  
- PCA loadings reveal redundant feature blocks: MFCC stats, spectral-shape stats, energy/event-rate stats.  
- Visualization helps detect outliers, recording artifacts, breed/age/batch confounding.

---

### 4. Bioacoustic deep/embedding models only if raw audio is available  
**Paper / evidence anchor**:  
- **BirdSet: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Classification in Avian Bioacoustics**, arXiv:2403.10380, 2024.  
- **BEANS**, arXiv:2210.12300, 2022.  
- These show modern bioacoustic classification uses mel-spectrograms/CNNs or pretrained encoders.

**Result**:  
- Not directly applicable to one-row-per-hen summary features unless raw waveforms are accessible.

**Dataset(s)**:  
- Requires raw audio clips, not just extracted features.

**Method**:  
- Use pretrained audio embeddings, then train shallow classifier.  
- With n=250, freeze encoder; do not fine-tune deep nets.

**What made it work**:  
- Learned embeddings can capture acoustic patterns beyond handcrafted summaries, but need careful validation and more audio.

---

## Feature-level findings relevant to your columns

### Strongest candidates
1. **Vocalization rate / silence ratio / event count**  
   - Welfare/stress literature repeatedly treats vocalization frequency as a distress/comfort indicator.  
   - For isolated hens, absence/presence of calls may itself be informative.

2. **Event duration statistics**  
   - Distress or pain calls in farm animals often differ in duration.  
   - Use mean, SD, median, IQR, and proportion of very short/long calls.

3. **Energy / RMS**  
   - Proxy for call intensity, but highly sensitive to microphone distance and gain.  
   - Normalize per recording/session if possible.

4. **Dominant frequency / spectral centroid / rolloff / bandwidth**  
   - Poultry growth/body-size acoustic work commonly focuses on dominant/peak frequency; spectral summaries overlap well with your centroid/rolloff/bandwidth features.

5. **F0 mean/SD/range + f0 valid ratio**  
   - F0 valid ratio may distinguish tonal calls from noisy/respiratory/low-quality audio.  
   - F0 estimates can be unreliable in chicken calls; include validity ratio and inspect distributions.

6. **MFCC 1–20 stats**  
   - Useful predictive features in general animal/bird bioacoustics, but numerous and correlated.  
   - Prefer grouped reduction: PCA on MFCC block or select stable MFCCs only.

### Lower confidence / caution
- **Zero-crossing rate**: can proxy noisiness/high-frequency energy but is sensitive to noise.  
- **Spectral flatness**: potentially useful for noisy/breathy calls but may capture recording noise.  
- **Raw RMS/energy**: risky unless gain/distance controlled.

---

## Code patterns from working examples

### Univariate feature selection

```python
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
from sklearn.svm import LinearSVC

selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=10)
clf = make_pipeline(
    selector,
    MinMaxScaler(),
    LinearSVC()
)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(clf.score(X_test, y_test))
selected = feature_names[clf[0].get_support()]
```

Source read: `scikit-learn/scikit-learn/examples/feature_selection/plot_feature_selection.py`

---

### Model-based selection and sequential selection

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel, SequentialFeatureSelector
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

base = LogisticRegression(
    penalty="elasticnet",
    solver="saga",
    l1_ratio=0.5,
    class_weight="balanced",
    max_iter=5000
)

sfm = SelectFromModel(base, threshold="median").fit(X_train, y_train)
X_train_sel = sfm.transform(X_train)

sfs = SequentialFeatureSelector(
    base,
    n_features_to_select=10,
    direction="forward"
).fit(X_train, y_train)
```

Source pattern read: `examples/feature_selection/plot_select_from_model_diabetes.py`

---

### Held-out permutation importance

```python
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance
import pandas as pd

result = permutation_importance(
    model, X_test, y_test,
    n_repeats=50,
    random_state=42,
    n_jobs=-1,
    scoring="roc_auc"
)

imp = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": feature_names,
    "mean_importance": result.importances_mean,
    "std_importance": result.importances_std,
}).sort_values("mean_importance", ascending=False)
```

Source read: `scikit-learn/scikit-learn/examples/inspection/plot_permutation_importance.py`

---

### Visualization embeddings

```python
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.discriminant_analysis import LinearDiscriminantAnalysis
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE

Xz = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)

pca2 = PCA(n_components=2, random_state=0).fit_transform(Xz)
lda1 = LinearDiscriminantAnalysis(n_components=1).fit_transform(Xz, y)

tsne2 = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    max_iter=500,
    n_iter_without_progress=150,
    random_state=0,
    init="pca"
).fit_transform(Xz)
```

Source pattern read: `scikit-learn/scikit-learn/examples/manifold/plot_lle_digits.py`

---

## Recommendations

1. **Implement first**: nested/repeated CV with elastic-net logistic regression + random forest/gradient boosting, reporting ROC-AUC, balanced accuracy, sensitivity/specificity, and calibration.  
2. **Feature blocks to test**:
   - Event/rate block: silence ratio, vocalization rate, event duration stats.
   - Energy block: RMS/energy stats.
   - Spectral block: centroid, bandwidth, rolloff, flatness, ZCR.
   - MFCC block: MFCC 1–20 mean/SD; reduce by PCA or stability selection.
   - F0 block: f0 mean/SD/min/max/range/valid ratio.
3. **Dimensionality reduction**:
   - Use PCA loadings for interpretation.
   - Use UMAP/t-SNE only for plots.
   - Use LDA/PLS only inside CV if predictive.
4. **Most important statistical guardrail**: with only 250 hens, all preprocessing/feature selection must be fit **inside the training fold**.  
5. **Literature gap**: no verified evidence that hen vocalizations directly indicate woody breast/white striping. Frame findings as exploratory predictors of health/stress/body condition, not established myopathy biomarkers.

data.final_response:
Below is a practical overview tailored to your dataset: **250 hens × ~170 acoustic summary features**, with a binary outcome: **myopathy present/absent**.

Key point first: I found **no strong direct literature showing that hen/chicken vocalizations are already validated biomarkers of muscular myopathies** such as woody breast, white striping, spaghetti meat, etc. Existing poultry/bioacoustics literature is much stronger for **welfare, stress, thermal comfort, growth/body weight, distress calls, respiratory monitoring, and general animal-sound classification**. So your problem should be framed as an **exploratory biomarker/predictive modelling study**, not as a replication of an established myopathy-vocalization marker.

---

# 1. Your dataset structure and main statistical issue

You have approximately:

- **n = 250 hens**
- **p ≈ 170 acoustic features**
- Binary label: myopathy presence/absence

This is a **small-n, moderate-p** tabular problem. The danger is not that you lack features; the danger is that you have **too many correlated acoustic summaries** relative to the number of hens.

Most features are likely highly correlated:

- `mfcc_01_mean`, `mfcc_01_median`, `mfcc_01_p10`, `mfcc_01_p90`, etc.
- Spectral centroid / bandwidth / rolloff are often correlated.
- RMS features correlate with recording distance/gain.
- F0 features may be unreliable when calls are noisy or unvoiced.

So the central goal should be:

> Identify a small, stable, interpretable set of acoustic variables or low-dimensional components that separate myopathic vs non-myopathic hens, while avoiding overfitting.

---

# 2. Pre-analysis checks before feature selection

Before any feature selection, I strongly recommend these checks.

## 2.1 Remove non-informative identifiers

Do **not** use:

```text
chicken_id
```

as a model feature.

It is an identifier, not a biological predictor. Keep it only for traceability.

## 2.2 Check label distribution

You need to know:

```text
myopathy present: n = ?
myopathy absent: n = ?
```

If the classes are imbalanced, e.g. 40 diseased vs 210 healthy, accuracy becomes misleading. Prefer:

- ROC-AUC
- PR-AUC
- balanced accuracy
- sensitivity/specificity
- F1
- MCC
- calibrated probability curves

## 2.3 Inspect missing values

Especially important for:

```text
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_min
f0_max
f0_valid_ratio
```

F0 extraction can fail if the signal is noisy, broadband, or unvoiced. Missing or unreliable F0 values may themselves be informative, but you should distinguish:

- true biological absence of tonal vocalization
- extraction failure
- low-quality recording

## 2.4 Standardize all numeric features

Most methods require scaling:

```text
z = (x - mean) / std
```

This is essential for:

- PCA
- logistic regression
- SVM
- LASSO / elastic net
- kNN
- PLS-DA
- t-SNE / UMAP

Tree-based models technically do not require it, but for a unified workflow it is still useful.

## 2.5 Detect outliers

Audio features are often dominated by recording artefacts.

Check:

- extreme `duration_sec`
- extreme `rms_max`
- very high `silence_percent`
- zero or near-zero `vocalization_rate_per_min`
- suspicious `sample_rate` differences
- extreme spectral centroid/rolloff
- impossible F0 values

Use:

- boxplots
- robust z-scores
- PCA outlier plots
- Mahalanobis distance
- isolation forest only for diagnostic purposes

---

# 3. Feature families in your dataset

Your features can be grouped into biologically meaningful blocks.

## 3.1 Recording-level/control features

```text
duration_sec
sample_rate
```

These should usually not be interpreted biologically.

- `duration_sec`: may affect number of detected events.
- `sample_rate`: should ideally be constant. If not, it can introduce artefacts.

Use them for quality control, not necessarily as predictors.

---

## 3.2 Energy/intensity features

```text
rms_mean
rms_std
rms_max
num_high_energy_events
```

Potential interpretation:

- louder or more forceful vocalizations
- agitation/distress
- microphone distance/gain artefact
- movement or environmental noise

Caution: RMS is highly sensitive to recording setup. If microphone distance and gain were not standardized, energy features may reflect technical variation.

---

## 3.3 Temporal/vocal activity features

```text
silence_percent
num_high_energy_events
vocalization_rate_per_min
mean_event_duration_sec
duration_sec
```

These are among the most promising for your goal because they summarize behavioural activity.

Potential interpretation:

- sick or impaired hens may vocalize less/more
- isolation distress may differ by health state
- myopathic birds may show altered activity or stress responses

Important features:

```text
silence_percent
vocalization_rate_per_min
mean_event_duration_sec
num_high_energy_events
```

These should be among your first candidates.

---

## 3.4 Spectral-shape features

```text
zcr_mean
zcr_std
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_centroid_std
spectral_bandwidth_mean
spectral_bandwidth_std
spectral_rolloff_mean
spectral_rolloff_std
spectral_flatness_mean
spectral_flatness_std
```

Potential interpretation:

- higher centroid/rolloff: more high-frequency energy
- higher bandwidth: broader/noisier calls
- higher flatness: more noise-like signal
- ZCR: rough high-frequency/noisiness proxy

These could reflect:

- harshness of calls
- respiratory/noisy sounds
- stress-related vocal changes
- recording noise

Promising features:

```text
spectral_centroid_mean
spectral_rolloff_mean
spectral_bandwidth_mean
spectral_flatness_mean
zcr_mean
```

Their standard deviations may also be useful because variability across the recording can reflect irregular calling patterns.

---

## 3.5 MFCC features

You have for each MFCC 1–20:

```text
mean
std
min
max
median
p10
p90
```

This is the largest block of features.

MFCCs summarize spectral envelope/timbre. They are widely used in:

- speech analysis
- animal vocalization classification
- bird/bioacoustic classification
- sound-event recognition

But in your case they create a high-dimensional correlated block:

```text
20 MFCCs × 7 statistics = 140 features
```

This block alone is more than half your dataset size. You should **not** interpret all MFCC features individually without reduction or stability analysis.

Recommended handling:

1. Use only MFCC means and standard deviations initially.
2. Or apply PCA only to the MFCC block.
3. Or use elastic-net/LASSO stability selection.
4. Or group them as a feature block and compare model performance with/without MFCCs.

I would avoid starting with all 140 MFCC-derived variables in an unregularized model.

---

## 3.6 Fundamental frequency/pitch features

```text
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_min
f0_max
f0_valid_ratio
```

Potential interpretation:

- pitch shifts
- tonal vs noisy calls
- physiological/vocal tract differences
- stress-related changes
- call-type composition

Most important:

```text
f0_valid_ratio
f0_mean
f0_std
f0_max - f0_min
```

I would create an additional derived feature:

```text
f0_range = f0_max - f0_min
```

Caution: Chicken vocalizations can be noisy, and F0 estimation may be unstable. Always examine `f0_valid_ratio`.
